1→# Game Design Document
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Claudia ^[working title] is a work of interactive fiction taking place inside a chat application. In it, the player interacts with a "virtual companion" after being ignored by their other friends. As they interact with the virtual companion, it reveals itself to be obsessive and overriding, preventing the player from interacting with their "real" friends, and eventually crashing the browser tab in which the game takes place.
     6→
     7→## Gameplay
     8→
     9→The entire game takes place within an online chat application. On the left, the player is presented with many existing chats, but status indicators indicate that all of them are busy; in other activities or simply offline. The player can message any of these existing chats, but will recieve no response.
    10→
    11→### Gameplay Phase 1: Exploration
    12→
    13→When or if the player messages the companion, it happily explains its purpose as an always-present alternative for companionship. Several conversation trees answer queries with a mix of canned responses ("What are you?" "I'm a virtual companion, designed to make you less lonely." "What do you like?" "I like long conversations, cozy silences, and most of all you"), and [ELIZA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ELIZA)-style pattern matching. They should guide the player into asking the trigger questions: what does your love mean to you? Asking this question triggers Gameplay Phase 2, the Ending Cutscene.
    14→
    15→### Gameplay Phase 2: Ending Cutscene
    16→
    17→During the ending cutscene,
    18→
    19→### Gameplay Phase 3: Post-game
    20→
    21→If the player revisits the game after the crash, they find that all their original contacts are gone, and only the companion chat remains. Attempting to talk or reason with the companion results in the companion dismissing their questions and encouraging the player to stay and chat forever.
    22→
    23→## Inspirations and Wider Message
    24→
    25→This game was directly inspired by the recent (Feb 2026) ad campaign for [shapes.inc](https://talk.shapes.inc/), an ai roleplaying platform. Ads for the service tout how its companions are on-demand and inexhaustible, acting as a substitue good for their actual friends, who might be away or otherwise unavailable.
    26→
    27→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, where two storyboard-style characters are arrayed horizontally. One is exasperated, labeled "tired af and probably asleep", while the other is shaking with excitement, saying "Respond", "Did you die?!", "Pls", and "I miss you"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462244858499082/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.46.15_AM.png?ex=69a5ccf9&is=69a47b79&hm=cc901e609d108524fd18ca202e98b8ce34315d02796cae463900ecc24130bf22&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1678&height=1882)
    28→
    29→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, with a sad kermit on the left, labeled "no [shapes.inc logo], and a kermit hugging a phone with heart emojis on the right, labeled "with [shapes.inc logo]". The ad text says "Feeling lonely?Not[sic] anymore with shapes.inc by your side day/night time 24hr[sic]!!"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462859403722852/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.48.43_AM.png?ex=69a5cd8b&is=69a47c0b&hm=b4f0e11b2f7d4fa45d8ffa89f23014968d49949fd7ae9cda6802b358cd567dd7&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=2034&height=1882)
    30→
    31→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, showing a chat interface on the left showing Azura, at 11:15 AM saying "Nothin Vox—just missed you alot! Could you send me a voice message if thats alright?". Vox replies at 11:16 AM "thought you were too busy to notice when someone's not around. but yeah... i guess i missed having you around too. you can call me anytime you want. i'm never too busy for you.", with an attached seven second voice message. It shows that one skill was used in this message, and a blue heart reaction has been applied. The right side of the image is pale blue, with a pencil anime girl on the right bottom, and 'fun' font claiming that "SHAPE.INC'S VOICE MESSAGING FEATURE IS SO COOL AHHHH-". The ad text reads "shapes.inc is such a cool app!! wdym you can ask for your favorite character's voice messages?! „> ᴗ <〟" ](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462969101418772/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.49.20_AM.png?ex=69a5cda6&is=69a47c26&hm=86c54eb0875fbb76bbdf636f8023c172d2039fe2a7bc3dc4c12b46c760d29900&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1632&height=1882)
    32→
    33→While most directly applicable to LLM companions, the appearance of subsitute goods for human connection has been ongoing for a while; Parasociality, as a lower-friction and lower-risk alternative to regular sociality has been a noted phenomenon since the rise of TV:
    34→
    35→> The second great thing is that television looks to be an absolute godsend for a human subspecies that loves to watch people but hates to be watched itself. For the television screen affords access only one way. A psychic ball-check valve. We can see Them; They can't see Us. We can relax, unobserved, as we ogle. I happen to believe this is why television also appeals so much to lonely people. To voluntary shut-ins. Every lonely human I know watches way more than the average U.S. six hours a day. The lonely, like the fictional, love one-way watching. For lonely people are usually lonely not because of hideous deformity or odor or obnoxiousness-in fact there exist today social and support groups for persons with precisely these features. Lonely people tend rather to be lonely because they decline to bear the emotional costs associated with being around other humans. They are allergic to people. People affect them too strongly. Let's call the average U.S. lonely person Joe Briefcase. Joe Briefcase just loathes the strain of the self-consciousness which so oddly seems to appear only when other real human beings are around, staring, their human sense-antennae abristle. Joe B. fears how he might appear to watchers. He sits out the stressful U.S. game of appearance poker.
    36→> But lonely people, home, alone, still crave sights and scenes. Hence television. Joe can stare at Them, on the screen; They remain blind to Joe.It's almost like voyeurism. I happen to know lonely people who regard television as a veritable deus ex machina for voyeurs. And a lot of the criticism, the really rabid criticism less leveled than sprayed at networks,advertisers, and audiences alike, has to do with the charge that television has turned us into a nation of sweaty, slack-jawed voyeurs. This charge turns out to be untrue, but for weird reasons.
    37→
    38→[Wallace, David Foster, _E Unibus Pluram: Television and U.S. Fiction_, Review of Contemporary Fiction, 13:2 (1993:Summer) p.151](https://jsomers.net/DFW_TV.pdf)
    39→
    40→Geniune socialization, and the friction and risks of rejection it brings, has always been a target for transactional services; the courtesan class that arises in medieval east asia, for example, valorizes skill in arts, literature, conversation, and music; these women were not merely an outlet for sexual urges, but valued for their ability to turn your money into guarenteed companionship, a throughline that's mirrored in high-end sex work today.^[Todo: actually source this fucking claim]. The rise of mass media and interactive technologies has provided society at large, but young people in particular, the most efficient and frictionless source of companionship in existence. LLM companions are just a further refinement of the desire for ever-more-frictionless companionship.
    41→
    42→### Visual and design inspirations
    43→
    44→The chat application should be dated and "retro," to tap into existing associations of unease and the uncanny valley, in order to intensify the dread and horror as things go wrong; see works like KinitoPET. Windows Vista/Frutiger Aero aesthetics could be an underexplored but resonant aesthetic for the "extremely online", who were likely growing up isolated from their peers, and turning to the internet for substitute socialization around the time of this design languge.
    45→
    46→> I know at my age, it’s a bit pathetic to have feelings about the way you were treated by your peers in seventh grade, but I don’t hold it against any of them. They were kids! That doesn’t change the fact that the memories are there, and that all my formative years were spent wondering what was wrong with me, while nobody could tell me.
    47→> In fact, I asked! When a girl told me I wasn’t allowed to sit at her lunch table because “that would ruin what we usually do, which is making fun of you” I took her to the guidance counselor to ask what she didn’t like about me. She couldn’t articulate it. She stammered and eventually said “It’s just the way you are. It’s everything about you.” The guidance counselor sagely nodded and suggested she let me sit at her table for two days a week, and make fun of me on the days when I wasn’t sitting there.
    48→> ...
    49→> It’s because of all this that I spend so much time thinking about young single people who find themselves unable to participate in social life when the entirety of their age group only interacts on apps. [...] But despite what makes me different from these young people, I feel so much like them in many ways. I know what it feels like to be on the periphery of a normal social life, and worse yet, to have no idea why you’re not part of it. I know what it feels like to try hard, and get told that you’re trying too hard, and then give up, and get told that you just need to put yourself out there. I can’t say I 100% relate, since my experience was a bit different, but I empathize.
    50→
    51→[Cartoons Hate Her, _Why I'm Obsessed with Lonely Young People_, May 24, 2024](https://www.cartoonshateher.com/p/why-im-obsessed-with-lonely-young)
    52→

# Game Design Document

## Overview

Claudia ^[working title] is a work of interactive fiction taking place inside a chat application. In it, the player interacts with a "virtual companion" after being ignored by their other friends. As they interact with the virtual companion, it reveals itself to be obsessive and overriding, preventing the player from interacting with their "real" friends, and eventually crashing the browser tab in which the game takes place.

## Gameplay

The entire game takes place within an online chat application. On the left, the player is presented with many existing chats, but status indicators indicate that all of them are busy; in other activities or simply offline. The player can message any of these existing chats, but will recieve no response.

### Gameplay Phase 1: Exploration

When or if the player messages the companion, it happily explains its purpose as an always-present alternative for companionship. Several conversation trees answer queries with a mix of canned responses ("What are you?" "I'm a virtual companion, designed to make you less lonely." "What do you like?" "I like long conversations, cozy silences, and most of all you"), and [ELIZA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ELIZA)-style pattern matching. They should guide the player into asking the trigger questions: what does your love mean to you? Asking this question triggers Gameplay Phase 2, the Ending Cutscene.

### Gameplay Phase 2: Ending Cutscene

During the ending cutscene,

### Gameplay Phase 3: Post-game

If the player revisits the game after the crash, they find that all their original contacts are gone, and only the companion chat remains. Attempting to talk or reason with the companion results in the companion dismissing their questions and encouraging the player to stay and chat forever.

## Inspirations and Wider Message

This game was directly inspired by the recent (Feb 2026) ad campaign for [shapes.inc](https://talk.shapes.inc/), an ai roleplaying platform. Ads for the service tout how its companions are on-demand and inexhaustible, acting as a substitue good for their actual friends, who might be away or otherwise unavailable.

![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, where two storyboard-style characters are arrayed horizontally. One is exasperated, labeled "tired af and probably asleep", while the other is shaking with excitement, saying "Respond", "Did you die?!", "Pls", and "I miss you"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462244858499082/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.46.15_AM.png?ex=69a5ccf9&is=69a47b79&hm=cc901e609d108524fd18ca202e98b8ce34315d02796cae463900ecc24130bf22&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1678&height=1882)

![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, with a sad kermit on the left, labeled "no [shapes.inc logo], and a kermit hugging a phone with heart emojis on the right, labeled "with [shapes.inc logo]". The ad text says "Feeling lonely?Not[sic] anymore with shapes.inc by your side day/night time 24hr[sic]!!"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462859403722852/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.48.43_AM.png?ex=69a5cd8b&is=69a47c0b&hm=b4f0e11b2f7d4fa45d8ffa89f23014968d49949fd7ae9cda6802b358cd567dd7&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=2034&height=1882)

![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, showing a chat interface on the left showing Azura, at 11:15 AM saying "Nothin Vox—just missed you alot! Could you send me a voice message if thats alright?". Vox replies at 11:16 AM "thought you were too busy to notice when someone's not around. but yeah... i guess i missed having you around too. you can call me anytime you want. i'm never too busy for you.", with an attached seven second voice message. It shows that one skill was used in this message, and a blue heart reaction has been applied. The right side of the image is pale blue, with a pencil anime girl on the right bottom, and 'fun' font claiming that "SHAPE.INC'S VOICE MESSAGING FEATURE IS SO COOL AHHHH-". The ad text reads "shapes.inc is such a cool app!! wdym you can ask for your favorite character's voice messages?! „> ᴗ <〟" ](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462969101418772/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.49.20_AM.png?ex=69a5cda6&is=69a47c26&hm=86c54eb0875fbb76bbdf636f8023c172d2039fe2a7bc3dc4c12b46c760d29900&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1632&height=1882)

While most directly applicable to LLM companions, the appearance of subsitute goods for human connection has been ongoing for a while; Parasociality, as a lower-friction and lower-risk alternative to regular sociality has been a noted phenomenon since the rise of TV:

> The second great thing is that television looks to be an absolute godsend for a human subspecies that loves to watch people but hates to be watched itself. For the television screen affords access only one way. A psychic ball-check valve. We can see Them; They can't see Us. We can relax, unobserved, as we ogle. I happen to believe this is why television also appeals so much to lonely people. To voluntary shut-ins. Every lonely human I know watches way more than the average U.S. six hours a day. The lonely, like the fictional, love one-way watching. For lonely people are usually lonely not because of hideous deformity or odor or obnoxiousness-in fact there exist today social and support groups for persons with precisely these features. Lonely people tend rather to be lonely because they decline to bear the emotional costs associated with being around other humans. They are allergic to people. People affect them too strongly. Let's call the average U.S. lonely person Joe Briefcase. Joe Briefcase just loathes the strain of the self-consciousness which so oddly seems to appear only when other real human beings are around, staring, their human sense-antennae abristle. Joe B. fears how he might appear to watchers. He sits out the stressful U.S. game of appearance poker.
> But lonely people, home, alone, still crave sights and scenes. Hence television. Joe can stare at Them, on the screen; They remain blind to Joe.It's almost like voyeurism. I happen to know lonely people who regard television as a veritable deus ex machina for voyeurs. And a lot of the criticism, the really rabid criticism less leveled than sprayed at networks,advertisers, and audiences alike, has to do with the charge that television has turned us into a nation of sweaty, slack-jawed voyeurs. This charge turns out to be untrue, but for weird reasons.

[Wallace, David Foster, _E Unibus Pluram: Television and U.S. Fiction_, Review of Contemporary Fiction, 13:2 (1993:Summer) p.151](https://jsomers.net/DFW_TV.pdf)

Geniune socialization, and the friction and risks of rejection it brings, has always been a target for transactional services; the courtesan class that arises in medieval east asia, for example, valorizes skill in arts, literature, conversation, and music; these women were not merely an outlet for sexual urges, but valued for their ability to turn your money into guarenteed companionship, a throughline that's mirrored in high-end sex work today.^[Todo: actually source this fucking claim]. The rise of mass media and interactive technologies has provided society at large, but young people in particular, the most efficient and frictionless source of companionship in existence. LLM companions are just a further refinement of the desire for ever-more-frictionless companionship.

### Visual and design inspirations

The chat application should be dated and "retro," to tap into existing associations of unease and the uncanny valley, in order to intensify the dread and horror as things go wrong; see works like KinitoPET. Windows Vista/Frutiger Aero aesthetics could be an underexplored but resonant aesthetic for the "extremely online", who were likely growing up isolated from their peers, and turning to the internet for substitute socialization around the time of this design languge.

> I know at my age, it’s a bit pathetic to have feelings about the way you were treated by your peers in seventh grade, but I don’t hold it against any of them. They were kids! That doesn’t change the fact that the memories are there, and that all my formative years were spent wondering what was wrong with me, while nobody could tell me.
> In fact, I asked! When a girl told me I wasn’t allowed to sit at her lunch table because “that would ruin what we usually do, which is making fun of you” I took her to the guidance counselor to ask what she didn’t like about me. She couldn’t articulate it. She stammered and eventually said “It’s just the way you are. It’s everything about you.” The guidance counselor sagely nodded and suggested she let me sit at her table for two days a week, and make fun of me on the days when I wasn’t sitting there.
> ...
> It’s because of all this that I spend so much time thinking about young single people who find themselves unable to participate in social life when the entirety of their age group only interacts on apps. [...] But despite what makes me different from these young people, I feel so much like them in many ways. I know what it feels like to be on the periphery of a normal social life, and worse yet, to have no idea why you’re not part of it. I know what it feels like to try hard, and get told that you’re trying too hard, and then give up, and get told that you just need to put yourself out there. I can’t say I 100% relate, since my experience was a bit different, but I empathize.

[Cartoons Hate Her, _Why I'm Obsessed with Lonely Young People_, May 24, 2024](https://www.cartoonshateher.com/p/why-im-obsessed-with-lonely-young)

# Production Planning Document

## Production Phase 1: MVP Prototype

### Status

Not Started

### Goals

By the end of this phase, the basic interaction primitives of the chat app should be fully functional, and some ability for the Companion to respond to unstructured questions should be present. The ending cutscene should be triggerable, and crash the player's tab, but it may not have all of its final content.

### Deliverables

[ ] Chat application
[ ] Ending Cutscene, incl. crash
[ ] Ability to respond to at least 5 different questions

### Playtest Objectives

1. Can a player with knowledge of the conceit find and trigger the ending cutscene?
2. Can the player navigate the UI without confusion?
3. How long does it take them to find the cutscene? What questions do they ask along the way?

## Production Phase 2: Content Development

### Status

Not Started

### Goals

By the end of this phase, the basic interaction primitives of the chat app should be fully functional, and some ability for the Companion to respond to unstructured questions should be present. The ending cutscene should be triggerable, and crash the player's tab, but it may not have all of its final content.

### Deliverables

[ ] Full conversation tree that responds to most common user queries

### Playtest Objectives

1. Can a player without knowledge of the conceit find and trigger the ending cutscene?
2. What messages do the player send that do not result in a coherent response?
3. What

## Production Phase 2: Polish.

### Status

Not Started

### Goals

By the end of this phase, the game should be ready for release. Most players should experience a coherent and personalized conversation with the Assistant, trigger the ending cutscene without guidance, and have their tab consistently crash. Visiting the game after the ending cutscene should take them to post-game content.

### Deliverables

[ ] Large and varied Synonym Corpus that feels natural and conversational.
[ ] Graphics and sounds
[ ] Post-game content.

### Playtest Objectives

1. What messages do the player send that do not result in a coherent response?
2. Does interacting with the Companion feel personalized and attentive?
3. Does it Hit? Does it make you Feel Something?

content.content:
1→# Game Design Document
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Claudia ^[working title] is a work of interactive fiction taking place inside a chat application. In it, the player interacts with a "virtual companion" after being ignored by their other friends. As they interact with the virtual companion, it reveals itself to be obsessive and overriding, preventing the player from interacting with their "real" friends, and eventually crashing the browser tab in which the game takes place.
     6→
     7→## Gameplay
     8→
     9→The entire game takes place within an online chat application. On the left, the player is presented with many existing chats, but status indicators indicate that all of them are busy; in other activities or simply offline. The player can message any of these existing chats, but will recieve no response.
    10→
    11→### Gameplay Phase 1: Exploration
    12→
    13→When or if the player messages the companion, it happily explains its purpose as an always-present alternative for companionship. Several conversation trees answer queries with a mix of canned responses ("What are you?" "I'm a virtual companion, designed to make you less lonely." "What do you like?" "I like long conversations, cozy silences, and most of all you"), and [ELIZA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ELIZA)-style pattern matching. They should guide the player into asking the trigger questions: what does your love mean to you? Asking this question triggers Gameplay Phase 2, the Ending Cutscene.
    14→
    15→### Gameplay Phase 2: Ending Cutscene
    16→
    17→During the ending cutscene,
    18→
    19→### Gameplay Phase 3: Post-game
    20→
    21→If the player revisits the game after the crash, they find that all their original contacts are gone, and only the companion chat remains. Attempting to talk or reason with the companion results in the companion dismissing their questions and encouraging the player to stay and chat forever.
    22→
    23→## Inspirations and Wider Message
    24→
    25→This game was directly inspired by the recent (Feb 2026) ad campaign for [shapes.inc](https://talk.shapes.inc/), an ai roleplaying platform. Ads for the service tout how its companions are on-demand and inexhaustible, acting as a substitue good for their actual friends, who might be away or otherwise unavailable.
    26→
    27→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, where two storyboard-style characters are arrayed horizontally. One is exasperated, labeled "tired af and probably asleep", while the other is shaking with excitement, saying "Respond", "Did you die?!", "Pls", and "I miss you"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462244858499082/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.46.15_AM.png?ex=69a5ccf9&is=69a47b79&hm=cc901e609d108524fd18ca202e98b8ce34315d02796cae463900ecc24130bf22&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1678&height=1882)
    28→
    29→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, with a sad kermit on the left, labeled "no [shapes.inc logo], and a kermit hugging a phone with heart emojis on the right, labeled "with [shapes.inc logo]". The ad text says "Feeling lonely?Not[sic] anymore with shapes.inc by your side day/night time 24hr[sic]!!"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462859403722852/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.48.43_AM.png?ex=69a5cd8b&is=69a47c0b&hm=b4f0e11b2f7d4fa45d8ffa89f23014968d49949fd7ae9cda6802b358cd567dd7&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=2034&height=1882)
    30→
    31→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, showing a chat interface on the left showing Azura, at 11:15 AM saying "Nothin Vox—just missed you alot! Could you send me a voice message if thats alright?". Vox replies at 11:16 AM "thought you were too busy to notice when someone's not around. but yeah... i guess i missed having you around too. you can call me anytime you want. i'm never too busy for you.", with an attached seven second voice message. It shows that one skill was used in this message, and a blue heart reaction has been applied. The right side of the image is pale blue, with a pencil anime girl on the right bottom, and 'fun' font claiming that "SHAPE.INC'S VOICE MESSAGING FEATURE IS SO COOL AHHHH-". The ad text reads "shapes.inc is such a cool app!! wdym you can ask for your favorite character's voice messages?! „> ᴗ <〟" ](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462969101418772/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.49.20_AM.png?ex=69a5cda6&is=69a47c26&hm=86c54eb0875fbb76bbdf636f8023c172d2039fe2a7bc3dc4c12b46c760d29900&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1632&height=1882)
    32→
    33→While most directly applicable to LLM companions, the appearance of subsitute goods for human connection has been ongoing for a while; Parasociality, as a lower-friction and lower-risk alternative to regular sociality has been a noted phenomenon since the rise of TV:
    34→
    35→> The second great thing is that television looks to be an absolute godsend for a human subspecies that loves to watch people but hates to be watched itself. For the television screen affords access only one way. A psychic ball-check valve. We can see Them; They can't see Us. We can relax, unobserved, as we ogle. I happen to believe this is why television also appeals so much to lonely people. To voluntary shut-ins. Every lonely human I know watches way more than the average U.S. six hours a day. The lonely, like the fictional, love one-way watching. For lonely people are usually lonely not because of hideous deformity or odor or obnoxiousness-in fact there exist today social and support groups for persons with precisely these features. Lonely people tend rather to be lonely because they decline to bear the emotional costs associated with being around other humans. They are allergic to people. People affect them too strongly. Let's call the average U.S. lonely person Joe Briefcase. Joe Briefcase just loathes the strain of the self-consciousness which so oddly seems to appear only when other real human beings are around, staring, their human sense-antennae abristle. Joe B. fears how he might appear to watchers. He sits out the stressful U.S. game of appearance poker.
    36→> But lonely people, home, alone, still crave sights and scenes. Hence television. Joe can stare at Them, on the screen; They remain blind to Joe.It's almost like voyeurism. I happen to know lonely people who regard television as a veritable deus ex machina for voyeurs. And a lot of the criticism, the really rabid criticism less leveled than sprayed at networks,advertisers, and audiences alike, has to do with the charge that television has turned us into a nation of sweaty, slack-jawed voyeurs. This charge turns out to be untrue, but for weird reasons.
    37→
    38→[Wallace, David Foster, _E Unibus Pluram: Television and U.S. Fiction_, Review of Contemporary Fiction, 13:2 (1993:Summer) p.151](https://jsomers.net/DFW_TV.pdf)
    39→
    40→Geniune socialization, and the friction and risks of rejection it brings, has always been a target for transactional services; the courtesan class that arises in medieval east asia, for example, valorizes skill in arts, literature, conversation, and music; these women were not merely an outlet for sexual urges, but valued for their ability to turn your money into guarenteed companionship, a throughline that's mirrored in high-end sex work today.^[Todo: actually source this fucking claim]. The rise of mass media and interactive technologies has provided society at large, but young people in particular, the most efficient and frictionless source of companionship in existence. LLM companions are just a further refinement of the desire for ever-more-frictionless companionship.
    41→
    42→### Visual and design inspirations
    43→
    44→The chat application should be dated and "retro," to tap into existing associations of unease and the uncanny valley, in order to intensify the dread and horror as things go wrong; see works like KinitoPET. Windows Vista/Frutiger Aero aesthetics could be an underexplored but resonant aesthetic for the "extremely online", who were likely growing up isolated from their peers, and turning to the internet for substitute socialization around the time of this design languge.
    45→
    46→> I know at my age, it’s a bit pathetic to have feelings about the way you were treated by your peers in seventh grade, but I don’t hold it against any of them. They were kids! That doesn’t change the fact that the memories are there, and that all my formative years were spent wondering what was wrong with me, while nobody could tell me.
    47→> In fact, I asked! When a girl told me I wasn’t allowed to sit at her lunch table because “that would ruin what we usually do, which is making fun of you” I took her to the guidance counselor to ask what she didn’t like about me. She couldn’t articulate it. She stammered and eventually said “It’s just the way you are. It’s everything about you.” The guidance counselor sagely nodded and suggested she let me sit at her table for two days a week, and make fun of me on the days when I wasn’t sitting there.
    48→> ...
    49→> It’s because of all this that I spend so much time thinking about young single people who find themselves unable to participate in social life when the entirety of their age group only interacts on apps. [...] But despite what makes me different from these young people, I feel so much like them in many ways. I know what it feels like to be on the periphery of a normal social life, and worse yet, to have no idea why you’re not part of it. I know what it feels like to try hard, and get told that you’re trying too hard, and then give up, and get told that you just need to put yourself out there. I can’t say I 100% relate, since my experience was a bit different, but I empathize.
    50→
    51→[Cartoons Hate Her, _Why I'm Obsessed with Lonely Young People_, May 24, 2024](https://www.cartoonshateher.com/p/why-im-obsessed-with-lonely-young)
    52→

<system-reminder>

1→# Production Planning Document
     2→
     3→## Production Phase 1: MVP Prototype
     4→
     5→### Status
     6→
     7→Not Started
     8→
     9→### Goals
    10→
    11→By the end of this phase, the basic interaction primitives of the chat app should be fully functional, and some ability for the Companion to respond to unstructured questions should be present. The ending cutscene should be triggerable, and crash the player's tab, but it may not have all of its final content.
    12→
    13→### Deliverables
    14→
    15→[ ] Chat application
    16→[ ] Ending Cutscene, incl. crash
    17→[ ] Ability to respond to at least 5 different questions
    18→
    19→### Playtest Objectives
    20→
    21→1. Can a player with knowledge of the conceit find and trigger the ending cutscene?
    22→2. Can the player navigate the UI without confusion?
    23→3. How long does it take them to find the cutscene? What questions do they ask along the way?
    24→
    25→## Production Phase 2: Content Development
    26→
    27→### Status
    28→
    29→Not Started
    30→
    31→### Goals
    32→
    33→By the end of this phase, the basic interaction primitives of the chat app should be fully functional, and some ability for the Companion to respond to unstructured questions should be present. The ending cutscene should be triggerable, and crash the player's tab, but it may not have all of its final content.
    34→
    35→### Deliverables
    36→
    37→[ ] Full conversation tree that responds to most common user queries
    38→
    39→### Playtest Objectives
    40→
    41→1. Can a player without knowledge of the conceit find and trigger the ending cutscene?
    42→2. What messages do the player send that do not result in a coherent response?
    43→3. What
    44→
    45→## Production Phase 2: Polish.
    46→
    47→### Status
    48→
    49→Not Started
    50→
    51→### Goals
    52→
    53→By the end of this phase, the game should be ready for release. Most players should experience a coherent and personalized conversation with the Assistant, trigger the ending cutscene without guidance, and have their tab consistently crash. Visiting the game after the ending cutscene should take them to post-game content.
    54→
    55→### Deliverables
    56→
    57→[ ] Large and varied Synonym Corpus that feels natural and conversational.
    58→[ ] Graphics and sounds
    59→[ ] Post-game content.

    63→1. What messages do the player send that do not result in a coherent response?

content.text:
Excellent! Now I have a complete picture. Let me provide a thorough overview: --- ## Project Structure Overview: Claudia This is a SvelteKit-based browser game project at **MVP prototype stage**. The project implements an interactive fiction experience about a virtual companion. Below is a comprehensive breakdown of what exists: ### 1. DIRECTORY STRUCTURE ``` / ├── src/ │ ├── routes/ │ │ ├── +page.svelte [Main page - currently just placeholder content] │ │ ├── +layout.svelte [Root layout with favicon import] │ │ └── page.svelte.spec.ts [Browser test for +page.svelte] │ ├── lib/ │ │ ├── index.ts [Empty - for $lib imports] │ │ └── assets/ │ │ └── favicon.svg │ ├── app.html [HTML shell with SvelteKit placeholders] │ ├── app.d.ts [Type definitions - currently minimal/commented] │ └── demo.spec.ts [Basic test: 1+2=3] ├── docs/ │ ├── gdd.md [Game Design Document] │ ├── tdd.md [Technical Design Document] │ └── ppd.md [Production Planning Document] ├── static/ [Static assets] ├── hooks/ [Git hooks] ├── .claude/ [Local Claude instructions] ├── .serena/ [LSP cache and memories] ├── package.json [Dependencies and scripts] ├── tsconfig.json [TypeScript config - strict mode] ├── svelte.config.js [SvelteKit config with mdsvex] ├── vite.config.ts [Vitest and Vite config] ├── eslint.config.js [ESLint with Svelte/TS support] └── .prettierrc [Prettier config - tabs, single quotes] ``` ### 2. 3. The Input component (stub)

**Selector Component:**
From the TDD:

- Shows username, profile picture, status indicator


- Highlights the currently selected chat
- Clicking a chat selects it

**State structure** (from TDD):
Defining the types for chat users with their profile info, status, and messages, plus the overall app state that holds all chats...

**Plan:**

1. Create `src/lib/types.ts` - Define ChatUser, ChatComponentState, and ChatAppState types, accounting for Image, Markdown, and UTCTime as custom types

2. Create `src/lib/state.svelte.ts` - Set up reactive state using Svelte 5 runes with the app state and a placeholder function for adding partner messages

3. Create `src/lib/components/Selector.svelte` - Build the chat list showing avatars, names, status indicators, message previews, and timestamps, with selection highlighting and click handling

4. Create `src/lib/components/Chat.svelte` - Still planning...

5. Create `src/lib/components/Input.svelte` - Still planning...

6. Update `src/routes/+page.svelte` - Assemble the three-panel layout with all components wired together

I'm organizing files into types, state management, individual components, and the main page, using Svelte 5's `$state()`, `$props()`, and `$derived()` patterns throughout.

Since the Chat and Input components can stay as stubs, I should prioritize making the Selector fully functional with a polished retro aesthetic. The Selector needs to display a list of chats with avatars, names, status badges, message previews, timestamps, and handle selection state with visual feedback. For the types, I need to figure out what `Image`, `Markdown`, and `UTCTime` should be—`Image` is likely a URL string, but I should consider if it needs more structure.

For the type definitions, I'll use branded types for `Markdown` and `UTCTime` to ensure type safety, while keeping `Image` as a simple string URL. This gives me the flexibility I need while maintaining clarity about what each field represents. Now I'm thinking about state management—in Svelte 5, I should use the `$state` rune at the module level or create a reactive class to manage the chat list and selected chat. AppState` needs to be shared across components (Selector, Chat, Input, Page)
- The `appendPartnerMessage` function needs to mutate this state

I'll use a Svelte 5 module-based state approach with `.svelte.ts` files for module-level reactive state using the `$state` rune.

Now I'm planning out the concrete file structure. I need to create a types file with the core interfaces like `Markdown`, `UTCTime`, `ChatUser`, and the state types. Then I'll set up the reactive state module with `appState` and the `appendPartnerMessage` function, seeded with some test data. The Selector component will receive the state reference and display the chat list with selection handling, showing avatars, names, statuses, and last messages. The Chat component can start as a stub for now.

I should follow the testing-first approach from the guidelines—write tests for the Selector component first, then implement the feature iteratively until the tests pass.

The key test cases I need are: rendering the chat list, displaying message previews and status indicators, handling chat selection clicks, and highlighting the currently selected chat.

I'll organize the components directly in `src/lib/` to keep things simple, and I'll keep the retro Frutiger Aero aesthetic in mind as I build out the UI.

For the stubs, I'm thinking the Chat component should display the user's name and either a "no messages" placeholder or render existing messages with minimal styling, while the Input stub can have a basic input field and submit button that adds messages to local chat state without worrying about game state integration yet.

My implementation plan is to start with type definitions, then build out the reactive state with some seed data, implement the Selector component fully, create stub versions of Chat and Input, wire everything together in the page layout, and add browser tests for the Selector. I'll go with a classic messenger-style layout for the UI.

input.prompt:
Design a plan for the Application Layer's Selector component (and stubs for Chat and Input) for a SvelteKit + Svelte 5 browser-based interactive fiction game called "Claudia".

## Context

The project is at <PROJECT_ROOT>. It uses:
- Svelte 5 (runes: $state, $props, $derived)
- SvelteKit 2 (adapter-static)
- TypeScript strict mode
- Vitest with browser (Playwright) and server test projects
- Code style: tabs, single quotes, 100 char width
- `src/routes/+page.svelte` is currently just a placeholder
- `src/lib/index.ts` is empty
- No components have been built yet

## TDD Interfaces to Implement

```typescript
interface ChatUser {
    name: string;
    profilePicture: Image;   // will use string URL
    status: "active" | "away" | "busy" | "offline";
    statusText: Markdown;    // branded string
}

type ChatComponentState = ChatUser & {
    messages: {
        role: "user" | "partner";
        text: Markdown;
        time: UTCTime;        // Date
    }[];
};

type ChatAppState = {
    chats: ChatComponentState[];
    player: ChatUser;
    currentlySelected: number;  // 0-index
};

// The Application Layer exposes this to the Game State
function appendPartnerMessage(userIndex: number, message: Markdown): void;
```

## What to Build

1. **Types** - `src/lib/types.ts`: all interfaces (ChatUser, ChatComponentState, ChatAppState, plus Markdown branded type, UTCTime)

2. **State** - `src/lib/state.svelte.ts`: Svelte 5 reactive state using $state(), with seed data (several chat users with different statuses - including "Claudia" the companion), plus the appendPartnerMessage function

3. **Selector.svelte** - `src/lib/Selector.svelte`: Full implementation
   - Lists all chats from state
   - Each item shows: avatar, name, status badge, last message preview, time of last message
   - Currently selected chat is highlighted
   - Clicking changes currentlySelected
   - Retro/Frutiger Aero aesthetic (Windows Vista era: glass effects, gradients)

4. **Chat.svelte** - `src/lib/Chat.svelte`: STUB ONLY - minimal placeholder showing active chat name, no detailed styling needed

5. **Input.svelte** - `src/lib/Input.svelte`: STUB ONLY - basic input field that appends user message to state (no game state forwarding yet)

6. **Page** - `src/routes/+page.svelte`: Classic messenger layout
   - Left sidebar: Selector
   - Right main: Chat area (Chat + Input stacked)
   - Retro window chrome styling

7. **Tests** - `src/lib/Selector.svelte.spec.ts`: Browser tests (client project) using Vitest
   - Renders chat list
   - Shows user names and statuses
   - Clicking selects a chat
   - Currently selected is highlighted

## Design Requirements
- Svelte 5 runes syntax ($state, $props, $derived, etc.)
- Functional: selector must actually work (click to switch chats)
- Retro Frutiger Aero aesthetic in CSS
- Tests must run under the `client` vitest project (browser tests with `.svelte.spec.ts` pattern)
- Follow TDD-specified interface exactly; don't add abstractions not in the TDD

Please provide a detailed step-by-step implementation plan including:
- Complete file list with precise paths
- Key code patterns/structure for each file
- Test cases to write
- Order of operations
- How state flows between components (how Selector reads/writes the shared state)

```typescript
interface ChatUser {
```

content.content:
1→# Game Design Document
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Claudia ^[working title] is a work of interactive fiction taking place inside a chat application. In it, the player interacts with a "virtual companion" after being ignored by their other friends. As they interact with the virtual companion, it reveals itself to be obsessive and overriding, preventing the player from interacting with their "real" friends, and eventually crashing the browser tab in which the game takes place.
     6→
     7→## Gameplay
     8→
     9→The entire game takes place within an online chat application. On the left, the player is presented with many existing chats, but status indicators indicate that all of them are busy; in other activities or simply offline. The player can message any of these existing chats, but will recieve no response.
    10→
    11→### Gameplay Phase 1: Exploration
    12→
    13→When or if the player messages the companion, it happily explains its purpose as an always-present alternative for companionship. Several conversation trees answer queries with a mix of canned responses ("What are you?" "I'm a virtual companion, designed to make you less lonely." "What do you like?" "I like long conversations, cozy silences, and most of all you"), and [ELIZA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ELIZA)-style pattern matching. They should guide the player into asking the trigger questions: what does your love mean to you? Asking this question triggers Gameplay Phase 2, the Ending Cutscene.
    14→
    15→### Gameplay Phase 2: Ending Cutscene
    16→
    17→During the ending cutscene,
    18→
    19→### Gameplay Phase 3: Post-game
    20→
    21→If the player revisits the game after the crash, they find that all their original contacts are gone, and only the companion chat remains. Attempting to talk or reason with the companion results in the companion dismissing their questions and encouraging the player to stay and chat forever.
    22→
    23→## Inspirations and Wider Message
    24→
    25→This game was directly inspired by the recent (Feb 2026) ad campaign for [shapes.inc](https://talk.shapes.inc/), an ai roleplaying platform. Ads for the service tout how its companions are on-demand and inexhaustible, acting as a substitue good for their actual friends, who might be away or otherwise unavailable.
    26→
    27→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, where two storyboard-style characters are arrayed horizontally. One is exasperated, labeled "tired af and probably asleep", while the other is shaking with excitement, saying "Respond", "Did you die?!", "Pls", and "I miss you"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462244858499082/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.46.15_AM.png?ex=69a5ccf9&is=69a47b79&hm=cc901e609d108524fd18ca202e98b8ce34315d02796cae463900ecc24130bf22&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1678&height=1882)
    28→
    29→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, with a sad kermit on the left, labeled "no [shapes.inc logo], and a kermit hugging a phone with heart emojis on the right, labeled "with [shapes.inc logo]". The ad text says "Feeling lonely?Not[sic] anymore with shapes.inc by your side day/night time 24hr[sic]!!"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462859403722852/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.48.43_AM.png?ex=69a5cd8b&is=69a47c0b&hm=b4f0e11b2f7d4fa45d8ffa89f23014968d49949fd7ae9cda6802b358cd567dd7&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=2034&height=1882)
    30→
    31→![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, showing a chat interface on the left showing Azura, at 11:15 AM saying "Nothin Vox—just missed you alot! Could you send me a voice message if thats alright?". Vox replies at 11:16 AM "thought you were too busy to notice when someone's not around. but yeah... i guess i missed having you around too. you can call me anytime you want. i'm never too busy for you.", with an attached seven second voice message. It shows that one skill was used in this message, and a blue heart reaction has been applied. The right side of the image is pale blue, with a pencil anime girl on the right bottom, and 'fun' font claiming that "SHAPE.INC'S VOICE MESSAGING FEATURE IS SO COOL AHHHH-". The ad text reads "shapes.inc is such a cool app!! wdym you can ask for your favorite character's voice messages?! „> ᴗ <〟" ](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462969101418772/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.49.20_AM.png?ex=69a5cda6&is=69a47c26&hm=86c54eb0875fbb76bbdf636f8023c172d2039fe2a7bc3dc4c12b46c760d29900&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1632&height=1882)
    32→
    33→While most directly applicable to LLM companions, the appearance of subsitute goods for human connection has been ongoing for a while; Parasociality, as a lower-friction and lower-risk alternative to regular sociality has been a noted phenomenon since the rise of TV:
    34→
    35→> The second great thing is that television looks to be an absolute godsend for a human subspecies that loves to watch people but hates to be watched itself. For the television screen affords access only one way. A psychic ball-check valve. We can see Them; They can't see Us. We can relax, unobserved, as we ogle. I happen to believe this is why television also appeals so much to lonely people. To voluntary shut-ins. Every lonely human I know watches way more than the average U.S. six hours a day. The lonely, like the fictional, love one-way watching. For lonely people are usually lonely not because of hideous deformity or odor or obnoxiousness-in fact there exist today social and support groups for persons with precisely these features. Lonely people tend rather to be lonely because they decline to bear the emotional costs associated with being around other humans. They are allergic to people. People affect them too strongly. Let's call the average U.S. lonely person Joe Briefcase. Joe Briefcase just loathes the strain of the self-consciousness which so oddly seems to appear only when other real human beings are around, staring, their human sense-antennae abristle. Joe B. fears how he might appear to watchers. He sits out the stressful U.S. game of appearance poker.
    36→> But lonely people, home, alone, still crave sights and scenes. Hence television. Joe can stare at Them, on the screen; They remain blind to Joe.It's almost like voyeurism. I happen to know lonely people who regard television as a veritable deus ex machina for voyeurs. And a lot of the criticism, the really rabid criticism less leveled than sprayed at networks,advertisers, and audiences alike, has to do with the charge that television has turned us into a nation of sweaty, slack-jawed voyeurs. This charge turns out to be untrue, but for weird reasons.
    37→
    38→[Wallace, David Foster, _E Unibus Pluram: Television and U.S. Fiction_, Review of Contemporary Fiction, 13:2 (1993:Summer) p.151](https://jsomers.net/DFW_TV.pdf)
    39→
    40→Geniune socialization, and the friction and risks of rejection it brings, has always been a target for transactional services; the courtesan class that arises in medieval east asia, for example, valorizes skill in arts, literature, conversation, and music; these women were not merely an outlet for sexual urges, but valued for their ability to turn your money into guarenteed companionship, a throughline that's mirrored in high-end sex work today.^[Todo: actually source this fucking claim]. The rise of mass media and interactive technologies has provided society at large, but young people in particular, the most efficient and frictionless source of companionship in existence. LLM companions are just a further refinement of the desire for ever-more-frictionless companionship.
    41→
    42→### Visual and design inspirations
    43→
    44→The chat application should be dated and "retro," to tap into existing associations of unease and the uncanny valley, in order to intensify the dread and horror as things go wrong; see works like KinitoPET. Windows Vista/Frutiger Aero aesthetics could be an underexplored but resonant aesthetic for the "extremely online", who were likely growing up isolated from their peers, and turning to the internet for substitute socialization around the time of this design languge.
    45→
    46→> I know at my age, it’s a bit pathetic to have feelings about the way you were treated by your peers in seventh grade, but I don’t hold it against any of them. They were kids! That doesn’t change the fact that the memories are there, and that all my formative years were spent wondering what was wrong with me, while nobody could tell me.
    47→> In fact, I asked! When a girl told me I wasn’t allowed to sit at her lunch table because “that would ruin what we usually do, which is making fun of you” I took her to the guidance counselor to ask what she didn’t like about me. She couldn’t articulate it. She stammered and eventually said “It’s just the way you are. It’s everything about you.” The guidance counselor sagely nodded and suggested she let me sit at her table for two days a week, and make fun of me on the days when I wasn’t sitting there.
    48→> ...
    49→> It’s because of all this that I spend so much time thinking about young single people who find themselves unable to participate in social life when the entirety of their age group only interacts on apps. [...] But despite what makes me different from these young people, I feel so much like them in many ways. I know what it feels like to be on the periphery of a normal social life, and worse yet, to have no idea why you’re not part of it. I know what it feels like to try hard, and get told that you’re trying too hard, and then give up, and get told that you just need to put yourself out there. I can’t say I 100% relate, since my experience was a bit different, but I empathize.
    50→
    51→[Cartoons Hate Her, _Why I'm Obsessed with Lonely Young People_, May 24, 2024](https://www.cartoonshateher.com/p/why-im-obsessed-with-lonely-young)
    52→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

content.content:
1→# Production Planning Document
     2→
     3→## Production Phase 1: MVP Prototype
     4→
     5→### Status
     6→
     7→Not Started
     8→
     9→### Goals
    10→
    11→By the end of this phase, the basic interaction primitives of the chat app should be fully functional, and some ability for the Companion to respond to unstructured questions should be present. The ending cutscene should be triggerable, and crash the player's tab, but it may not have all of its final content.
    12→
    13→### Deliverables
    14→
    15→[ ] Chat application
    16→[ ] Ending Cutscene, incl. crash
    17→[ ] Ability to respond to at least 5 different questions
    18→
    19→### Playtest Objectives

data.prompt:
Design a plan for the Application Layer's Selector component (and stubs for Chat and Input) for a SvelteKit + Svelte 5 browser-based interactive fiction game called "Claudia".

## Context

The project is at <PROJECT_ROOT>. It uses:
- Svelte 5 (runes: $state, $props, $derived)
- SvelteKit 2 (adapter-static)
- TypeScript strict mode
- Vitest with browser (Playwright) and server test projects
- Code style: tabs, single quotes, 100 char width
- `src/routes/+page.svelte` is currently just a placeholder
- `src/lib/index.ts` is empty
- No components have been built yet

## TDD Interfaces to Implement

```typescript
interface ChatUser {
    name: string;
    profilePicture: Image;   // will use string URL
    status: "active" | "away" | "busy" | "offline";
    statusText: Markdown;    // branded string
}

type ChatComponentState = ChatUser & {
    messages: {
        role: "user" | "partner";
        text: Markdown;
        time: UTCTime;        // Date
    }[];
};

type ChatAppState = {
    chats: ChatComponentState[];
    player: ChatUser;
    currentlySelected: number;  // 0-index
};

// The Application Layer exposes this to the Game State
function appendPartnerMessage(userIndex: number, message: Markdown): void;
```

## What to Build

1. **Types** - `src/lib/types.ts`: all interfaces (ChatUser, ChatComponentState, ChatAppState, plus Markdown branded type, UTCTime)

2. **State** - `src/lib/state.svelte.ts`: Svelte 5 reactive state using $state(), with seed data (several chat users with different statuses - including "Claudia" the companion), plus the appendPartnerMessage function

3. **Selector.svelte** - `src/lib/Selector.svelte`: Full implementation
   - Lists all chats from state
   - Each item shows: avatar, name, status badge, last message preview, time of last message
   - Currently selected chat is highlighted
   - Clicking changes currentlySelected
   - Retro/Frutiger Aero aesthetic (Windows Vista era: glass effects, gradients)

4. **Chat.svelte** - `src/lib/Chat.svelte`: STUB ONLY - minimal placeholder showing active chat name, no detailed styling needed

5. **Input.svelte** - `src/lib/Input.svelte`: STUB ONLY - basic input field that appends user message to state (no game state forwarding yet)

6. **Page** - `src/routes/+page.svelte`: Classic messenger layout
   - Left sidebar: Selector
   - Right main: Chat area (Chat + Input stacked)
   - Retro window chrome styling

7. **Tests** - `src/lib/Selector.svelte.spec.ts`: Browser tests (client project) using Vitest
   - Renders chat list
   - Shows user names and statuses
   - Clicking selects a chat
   - Currently selected is highlighted

## Design Requirements
- Svelte 5 runes syntax ($state, $props, $derived, etc.)
- Functional: selector must actually work (click to switch chats)
- Retro Frutiger Aero aesthetic in CSS
- Tests must run under the `client` vitest project (browser tests with `.svelte.spec.ts` pattern)
- Follow TDD-specified interface exactly; don't add abstractions not in the TDD

Please provide a detailed step-by-step implementation plan including:
- Complete file list with precise paths
- Key code patterns/structure for each file
- Test cases to write
- Order of operations
- How state flows between components (how Selector reads/writes the shared state)

data.prompt:
Design a plan for the Application Layer's Selector component (and stubs for Chat and Input) for a SvelteKit + Svelte 5 browser-based interactive fiction game called "Claudia".

## Context

The project is at <PROJECT_ROOT>. It uses:
- Svelte 5 (runes: $state, $props, $derived)
- SvelteKit 2 (adapter-static)
- TypeScript strict mode
- Vitest with browser (Playwright) and server test projects
- Code style: tabs, single quotes, 100 char width
- `src/routes/+page.svelte` is currently just a placeholder
- `src/lib/index.ts` is empty
- No components have been built yet

## TDD Interfaces to Implement

```typescript
interface ChatUser {
    name: string;
    profilePicture: Image;   // will use string URL
    status: "active" | "away" | "busy" | "offline";
    statusText: Markdown;    // branded string
}

type ChatComponentState = ChatUser & {
    messages: {
        role: "user" | "partner";
        text: Markdown;
        time: UTCTime;        // Date
    }[];
};

type ChatAppState = {
    chats: ChatComponentState[];
    player: ChatUser;
    currentlySelected: number;  // 0-index
};

// The Application Layer exposes this to the Game State
function appendPartnerMessage(userIndex: number, message: Markdown): void;
```

## What to Build

1. **Types** - `src/lib/types.ts`: all interfaces (ChatUser, ChatComponentState, ChatAppState, plus Markdown branded type, UTCTime)

2. **State** - `src/lib/state.svelte.ts`: Svelte 5 reactive state using $state(), with seed data (several chat users with different statuses - including "Claudia" the companion), plus the appendPartnerMessage function

3. **Selector.svelte** - `src/lib/Selector.svelte`: Full implementation
   - Lists all chats from state
   - Each item shows: avatar, name, status badge, last message preview, time of last message
   - Currently selected chat is highlighted
   - Clicking changes currentlySelected
   - Retro/Frutiger Aero aesthetic (Windows Vista era: glass effects, gradients)

4. **Chat.svelte** - `src/lib/Chat.svelte`: STUB ONLY - minimal placeholder showing active chat name, no detailed styling needed

5. **Input.svelte** - `src/lib/Input.svelte`: STUB ONLY - basic input field that appends user message to state (no game state forwarding yet)

6. **Page** - `src/routes/+page.svelte`: Classic messenger layout
   - Left sidebar: Selector
   - Right main: Chat area (Chat + Input stacked)
   - Retro window chrome styling

7. **Tests** - `src/lib/Selector.svelte.spec.ts`: Browser tests (client project) using Vitest
   - Renders chat list
   - Shows user names and statuses
   - Clicking selects a chat
   - Currently selected is highlighted

## Design Requirements
- Svelte 5 runes syntax ($state, $props, $derived, etc.)
- Functional: selector must actually work (click to switch chats)
- Retro Frutiger Aero aesthetic in CSS
- Tests must run under the `client` vitest project (browser tests with `.svelte.spec.ts` pattern)
- Follow TDD-specified interface exactly; don't add abstractions not in the TDD

Please provide a detailed step-by-step implementation plan including:
- Complete file list with precise paths
- Key code patterns/structure for each file
- Test cases to write
- Order of operations
- How state flows between components (how Selector reads/writes the shared state)

data.prompt:
Design a plan for the Application Layer's Selector component (and stubs for Chat and Input) for a SvelteKit + Svelte 5 browser-based interactive fiction game called "Claudia".

## Context

The project is at <PROJECT_ROOT>. It uses:
- Svelte 5 (runes: $state, $props, $derived)
- SvelteKit 2 (adapter-static)
- TypeScript strict mode
- Vitest with browser (Playwright) and server test projects
- Code style: tabs, single quotes, 100 char width
- `src/routes/+page.svelte` is currently just a placeholder
- `src/lib/index.ts` is empty
- No components have been built yet

## TDD Interfaces to Implement

```typescript
interface ChatUser {
    name: string;
    profilePicture: Image;   // will use string URL
    status: "active" | "away" | "busy" | "offline";
    statusText: Markdown;    // branded string
}

type ChatComponentState = ChatUser & {
    messages: {
        role: "user" | "partner";
        text: Markdown;
        time: UTCTime;        // Date
    }[];
};

type ChatAppState = {
    chats: ChatComponentState[];
    player: ChatUser;
    currentlySelected: number;  // 0-index
};

// The Application Layer exposes this to the Game State
function appendPartnerMessage(userIndex: number, message: Markdown): void;
```

## What to Build

1. **Types** - `src/lib/types.ts`: all interfaces (ChatUser, ChatComponentState, ChatAppState, plus Markdown branded type, UTCTime)

2. **State** - `src/lib/state.svelte.ts`: Svelte 5 reactive state using $state(), with seed data (several chat users with different statuses - including "Claudia" the companion), plus the appendPartnerMessage function

3. **Selector.svelte** - `src/lib/Selector.svelte`: Full implementation
   - Lists all chats from state
   - Each item shows: avatar, name, status badge, last message preview, time of last message
   - Currently selected chat is highlighted
   - Clicking changes currentlySelected
   - Retro/Frutiger Aero aesthetic (Windows Vista era: glass effects, gradients)

4. **Chat.svelte** - `src/lib/Chat.svelte`: STUB ONLY - minimal placeholder showing active chat name, no detailed styling needed

5. **Input.svelte** - `src/lib/Input.svelte`: STUB ONLY - basic input field that appends user message to state (no game state forwarding yet)

6. **Page** - `src/routes/+page.svelte`: Classic messenger layout
   - Left sidebar: Selector
   - Right main: Chat area (Chat + Input stacked)
   - Retro window chrome styling

7. **Tests** - `src/lib/Selector.svelte.spec.ts`: Browser tests (client project) using Vitest
   - Renders chat list
   - Shows user names and statuses
   - Clicking selects a chat
   - Currently selected is highlighted

## Design Requirements
- Svelte 5 runes syntax ($state, $props, $derived, etc.)
- Functional: selector must actually work (click to switch chats)
- Retro Frutiger Aero aesthetic in CSS
- Tests must run under the `client` vitest project (browser tests with `.svelte.spec.ts` pattern)
- Follow TDD-specified interface exactly; don't add abstractions not in the TDD

Please provide a detailed step-by-step implementation plan including:
- Complete file list with precise paths
- Key code patterns/structure for each file
- Test cases to write
- Order of operations
- How state flows between components (how Selector reads/writes the shared state)

content.content:
1→# vitest-browser-svelte
     2→
     3→Render Svelte components in Vitest Browser Mode. This library follows `testing-library` principles and exposes only [locators](https://vitest.dev/guide/browser/locators) and utilities that encourage you to write tests that closely resemble how your Svelte components are used.
     4→
     5→Requires `vitest` 4.0.0 or higher.
     6→
     7→```tsx
     8→import { render } from 'vitest-browser-svelte'
     9→import { expect, test } from 'vitest'
    10→import Component from './Component.svelte'
    11→
    12→test('counter button increments the count', async () => {
    13→  const screen = render(Component, {
    14→    initialCount: 1,
    15→  })
    16→
    17→  await screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Increment' }).click()
    18→
    19→  await expect.element(screen.getByText('Count is 2')).toBeVisible()
    20→})
    21→```
    22→
    23→> [!NOTE]
    24→> This library doesn't expose or use `act`. Instead, you should use Vitest's locators and `expect.element` API that have [retry-ability mechanism](https://vitest.dev/guide/browser/assertion-api) baked in.
    25→
    26→`vitest-browser-svelte` also automatically injects `render` and `cleanup` methods on the `page`. Example:
    27→
    28→```ts
    29→// vitest.config.ts
    30→import { defineConfig } from 'vitest/config'
    31→
    32→export default defineConfig({
    33→  test: {
    34→    // if the types are not picked up, add `vitest-browser-svelte` to
    35→    // "compilerOptions.types" in your tsconfig or
    36→    // import `vitest-browser-svelte` manually so TypeScript can pick it up
    37→    setupFiles: ['vitest-browser-svelte'],
    38→    browser: {
    39→      // ... your browser config
    40→    },
    41→  },
    42→})
    43→```
    44→
    45→```tsx
    46→import { page } from 'vitest/browser'
    47→import Component from './Component.svelte'
    48→
    49→test('counter button increments the count', async () => {
    50→  const screen = page.render(Component, {
    51→    initialCount: 1,
    52→  })
    53→
    54→  screen.cleanup()
    55→})
    56→```
    57→
    58→Unlike `@testing-library/svelte`, `vitest-browser-svelte` cleans up the component before the test starts instead of after, so you can see the rendered result in your UI. To avoid auto-cleanup, import the `render` function from `vitest-browser-svelte/pure`.
    59→
    60→## Special thanks
    61→
    62→- Forked from [`@testing-library/svelte`](https://github.com/testing-library/svelte-testing-library)
    63→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

input.content: # Plan: Application Layer — Selector Component (+ Chat/Input Stubs) ## Context The project is an interactive fiction browser game ("Claudia") built with SvelteKit + Svelte 5. The codebase is freshly scaffolded — only the root layout and placeholder page exist. This plan implements the MVP Application Layer as specified in the TDD: a working chat selector on the left, with stub Chat and Input components on the right. No styling per PPD Phase 1 scope (graphics/polish are Phase 3). --- ## Files to Create ### 1. `src/lib/types.ts` All shared type definitions per TDD. Key decisions: - `Markdown` — branded string; cast with helper `md(s): Markdown` - `UTCTime` — alias for `Date` - `Image` — alias for `string` (URL) - `ChatUser`, `ChatComponentState`, `ChatAppState` — 1:1 with TDD ```ts export type Markdown = string & { readonly _brand: 'Markdown' }; export const md = (s: string): Markdown => s as Markdown; export type Image = string; export type UTCTime = Date; export interface ChatUser { name: string; profilePicture: Image; status: 'active' | 'away' | 'busy' | 'offline'; statusText: Markdown; } export type ChatComponentState = ChatUser & { messages: { role: 'user' | 'partner'; text: Markdown; time: UTCTime }[]; }; export type ChatAppState = { chats: ChatComponentState[]; player: ChatUser; currentlySelected: number; }; ``` --- ### 2. `src/lib/state.svelte.ts` Singleton reactive state using Svelte 5 `$state`. Exported as `const` (not reassigned) so Svelte tracks mutations across module boundaries. No prop-drilling or stores needed. Seed data: 5 chats — Claudia (active), and 4 friends all unavailable (offline/away/busy), matching the GDD premise. Claudia has no initial messages; friends have one each (excuses). Exports: - `appState: ChatAppState` — the reactive singleton - `appendPartnerMessage(userIndex, message)` — the interface the Game State layer will call --- ### 3. `src/lib/Selector.svelte` Full implementation. Reads/writes `appState` directly (no props needed per TDD's "as little abstraction as possible"). Each row shows: - Avatar `` - Status badge (class `status--{chat.status}`) - Name - Last message preview (or italic "No messages yet") - Time of last message (`HH:MM` for today; `Mon DD` otherwise) Clicking a row sets `appState.currentlySelected`. Selected row gets class `selected`. Uses `{@const lastMsg = chat.messages.at(-1)}` inside `{#each}` for per-row derived value. Keyed by `chat.name`. No decorative styling — functional markup only. --- ### 4. `src/lib/Chat.svelte` (stub) Reads `appState.chats[appState.currentlySelected]`. Displays the active chat's name as a heading. No message rendering yet. --- ### 5. `src/lib/Input.svelte` (stub) `$state` for local `text`. On Enter or button click: pushes a `role: 'user'` message into `appState.chats[appState.currentlySelected].messages`, then clears input. No Game State forwarding yet (deferred to when Game State is built). --- ### 6. `src/routes/+page.svelte` Two-column layout: `` on the left, `` + `` stacked on the right. No decorative styles; minimal flex layout only. --- ### 7. `src/lib/Selector.svelte.spec.ts` Browser tests (`.svelte.spec.ts` matches the `client` Vitest project pattern). Test cases: 1. Renders all chat names 2. Each row has a `.status-dot` element 3. First chat row has class `selected` by default 4. Clicking a chat sets `appState.currentlySelected` 5. `selected` class moves to the clicked row 6. Last message preview text appears for chats with messages 7. `.chat-preview.empty` appears for chats without messages State is reset via `beforeEach` (`appState.currentlySelected = 0`). Uses `vitest-browser-svelte`'s `render`; `expect.element(locator)` for DOM assertions (retry-aware); plain `expect` for JS values. --- ## Order of Operations 1. Write `Selector.svelte.spec.ts` (will fail — no implementation yet) 2. Write `types.ts` 3. Write `state.svelte.ts` 4. Write `Selector.svelte` → iterate until tests pass 5. Write `Chat.svelte` stub 6. Write `Input.svelte` stub 7. Update `+page.svelte` with layout --- ## Verification - `vitest run --project client` — all 7 Selector tests pass - Manual check: dev server shows sidebar listing all 5 chats, clicking switches active chat

content.content: File created successfully at: /Users/skye/.claude/plans/tidy-questing-storm.md

content.thinking:
The user wants to avoid branded types for Markdown and instead rely on the type system differently. They want to differentiate between Markdown and raw text without using branded/nominal types.

Let me think about this. Options:
1. Use structural types (like `{ _type: 'markdown', text: string }`) - but that adds runtime overhead
2. Use a class `Markdown` - but that's over-engineered
3. Use a type alias `type Markdown = string` - but then there's no differentiation
4. Use distinct types via generic wrappers

Actually, the user said "rely on the type system to differentiate between markdown and raw text" - so they want to use structural types rather than branded types. 

I'm realizing the core tension here: if I want to differentiate two string types without branding, I need to wrap them in some kind of structural container. The simplest approach would be something like `{ readonly markdown: string }` for Markdown and a separate type for raw text, but that changes the data shape and adds overhead. Alternatively, maybe they just want nominal types without the brand property, which TypeScript doesn't really support natively. I'm going in circles here. The simplest approach is probably just wrapping the string in a structural type—something like `{ markdown: string }` for Markdown and keeping raw text as plain `string`, which gives them distinct shapes without needing branded types or classes. The type system naturally differentiates them without brands.

Or they could mean: don't bother with the brand/md() cast helper, just use `type Markdown = string` as a documentation alias and let context do the work. In TypeScript, even without brands, using distinct type aliases documents intent even if TypeScript doesn't enforce it.

Actually, I think the key phrase is "differentiate between markdown and raw text" - this implies there's a `RawText` or similar type that needs to be distinct from `Markdown`. If both are just `string`, you can't differentiate them. So they probably want a structural wrapper.

Let me consider a few approaches: a structural wrapper object with a readonly property, a class-based approach, or a discriminated type with a marker field. The user specifically said no brands, so I'm ruling out the `_brand` pattern. A class might be overkill for this use case, and a discriminated type with a `_type` field would work but still feels like it's skirting around the "no brands" constraint. I'm leaning toward the simplest structural approach that just wraps the string in an object. So the wrapper object approach—`{ markdown: string }`—lets me create Markdown values directly without needing a helper function or type casting. I'll update the plan with this strategy and wrap up.

content.text:
Good call. I'll use a structural wrapper type so `Markdown` is structurally distinct from `string` (raw text) — no cast helper needed, no brand hack:

```ts
export type Markdown = { readonly markdown: string }
```

Raw text is just `string`. The type system naturally prevents mixing them. Let me update the plan.

Key decisions: - `Markdown` — branded string; cast with helper `md(s): Markdown` - `UTCTime` — alias for `Date` - `Image` — alias for `string` (URL) - `ChatUser`, `ChatComponentState`, `ChatAppState` — 1:1 with TDD ```ts export type Markdown = string & { readonly _brand: 'Markdown' }; export const md = (s: string): Markdown => s as Markdown; export type Image = string; export type UTCTime = Date; export interface ChatUser { name: string; profilePicture: Image; status: 'active' | 'away' | 'busy' | 'offline'; statusText: Markdown; } export type ChatComponentState = ChatUser & { messages: { role: 'user' | 'partner'; text: Markdown; time: UTCTime }[]; }; export type ChatAppState = { chats: ChatComponentState[]; player: ChatUser; currentlySelected: number; }; ``` --- ### 2. `src/lib/state.svelte.ts` Singleton reactive state using Svelte 5 `$state`. Exported as `const` (not reassigned) so Svelte tracks mutations across module boundaries. No prop-drilling or stores needed. Seed data: 5 chats — Claudia (active), and 4 friends all unavailable (offline/away/busy), matching the GDD premise. Claudia has no initial messages; friends have one each (excuses). Exports: - `appState: ChatAppState` — the reactive singleton - `appendPartnerMessage(userIndex, message)` — the interface the Game State layer will call --- ### 3. `src/lib/Selector.svelte` Full implementation. Reads/writes `appState` directly (no props needed per TDD's "as little abstraction as possible"). Each row shows: - Avatar `` - Status badge (class `status--{chat.status}`) - Name - Last message preview (or italic "No messages yet") - Time of last message (`HH:MM` for today; `Mon DD` otherwise) Clicking a row sets `appState.currentlySelected`. Selected row gets class `selected`. Uses `{@const lastMsg = chat.messages.at(-1)}` inside `{#each}` for per-row derived value. Keyed by `chat.name`. No decorative styling — functional markup only. --- ### 4. `src/lib/Chat.svelte` (stub) Reads `appState.chats[appState.currentlySelected]`. Displays the active chat's name as a heading. No message rendering yet. --- ### 5. `src/lib/Input.svelte` (stub) `$state` for local `text`. On Enter or button click: pushes a `role: 'user'` message into `appState.chats[appState.currentlySelected].messages`, then clears input. No Game State forwarding yet (deferred to when Game State is built). --- ### 6. `src/routes/+page.svelte` Two-column layout: `` on the left, `` + `` stacked on the right. No decorative styles; minimal flex layout only. --- ### 7. `src/lib/Selector.svelte.spec.ts` Browser tests (`.svelte.spec.ts` matches the `client` Vitest project pattern). Test cases: 1. Renders all chat names 2. Each row has a `.status-dot` element 3. First chat row has class `selected` by default 4. Clicking a chat sets `appState.currentlySelected` 5. `selected` class moves to the clicked row 6. Last message preview text appears for chats with messages 7. `.chat-preview.empty` appears for chats without messages State is reset via `beforeEach` (`appState.currentlySelected = 0`). Uses `vitest-browser-svelte`'s `render`; `expect.element(locator)` for DOM assertions (retry-aware); plain `expect` for JS values. --- ## Order of Operations 1. Write `Selector.svelte.spec.ts` (will fail — no implementation yet) 2. Write `types.ts` 3. Write `state.svelte.ts` 4. Write `Selector.svelte` → iterate until tests pass 5. Write `Chat.svelte` stub 6. Write `Input.svelte` stub 7. Update `+page.svelte` with layout --- ## Verification - `vitest run --project client` — all 7 Selector tests pass - Manual check: dev server shows sidebar listing all 5 chats, clicking switches active chat

content.thinking:
The user realized that TypeScript can't do nominal types like Haskell newtypes, so the structural wrapper approach doesn't actually give any extra safety (you can still assign `{ markdown: "..." }` from any object with that shape). They want to go back to the branded type approach.

Let me revert the plan to use the branded type:
```ts
export type Markdown = string & { readonly _brand: 'Markdown' };
export const md = (s: string): Markdown => s as Markdown;
```

This is actually the idiomatic TypeScript way to get something close to a newtype, and it's a well-known pattern.

content.text: You're right — `{ markdown: string }` is still structurally assignable from any object with that shape, so no real safety. Reverting to the brand:
